sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=51, col=12).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 4, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking the purple square at its current position doesn't move it - only the bottom bar decreased (62→60). The white cross was a one-time target marker. Need to find how to advance the purple square further along the path.
- Goal model: Move the purple square along the diagonal path toward the blue creature.
- Action model: Clicking the cross moved the purple square there. Clicking the square itself just wastes a step. Need to try clicking on the next path position.
- Plan: Try clicking on the next gray dot along the path toward the blue creature - position (51, 12).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 3 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after clicking on (51, 12). The board changed, so something happened. Let me see if the purple square moved.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
after = current_frame.segmentation
purple = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'p']
print("Purple objects:")
for n in purple:
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Bottom bar
bar = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 50]
print(f"\nBottom bar: {bar[0]['pixels'] if bar else 'N/A'} pixels")

# Gray dots
gray = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 1]
positions = sorted([(n['boundary'][0][0], n['boundary'][0][1]) for n in gray])
print(f"\nGray dots: {len(gray)}")
print(f"Positions: {positions}")

# Check for white cross
white_small = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] < 10]
print(f"\nSmall white objects: {len(white_small)}")
for n in white_small:
    print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The purple square moved from (52,9)-(54,11) to (50,11)-(52,13). That's a shift of (-2, +2) - moving up and right along the diagonal path toward the blue creature!
Also note that the gray dot that was at (51,12) is now gone, and a new gray dot appeared at (53,10) - the position where the purple square was before. So the purple square replaced the gray dot at (51,12), and the old position became a gray dot.
So the mechanic is:
1. Click on a gray dot along the path to move the purple square to that position
2. The purple square swaps with the gray dot (old position becomes a gray dot)
3. The bottom bar decreases by 2 each action
The path goes from bottom-left to top-right. The purple square is now at (51,12) approximately. The next gray dot toward the blue creature would be (49,14).
Let me continue clicking on each successive gray dot to move the purple square all the way to the blue creature. The path positions are:
(57,6), (55,8), (53,10), (49,14), (47,16), (45,18), (43,20), (41,22), (39,24), (37,26), (35,28), (33,30), (31,32), (29,34), (27,36), (25,38), (23,40), (21,42), (19,44), (17,46)
The purple square is currently at (51,12) which corresponds to the position between (53,10) and (49,14). The next target is (49,14).
Wait, let me reconsider. The purple square boundary is [[50, 11], [50, 13], [52, 13], [52, 11]], so it's a 3x3 square centered at (51, 12). The gray dot positions now include (53,10) which was the previous position of the purple square.
So the pattern is:
- Purple square was at (53,10) center
- I clicked on (51,12) which was a gray dot
- Purple square moved to (51,12) center
- The old position (53,10) became a gray dot
Now the purple square is at (51,12). The next gray dot toward the blue creature is (49,14). Let me click on that.
Actually, I should batch multiple clicks to be efficient. The path from current position to the blue creature goes through:
(49,14), (47,16), (45,18), (43,20), (41,22), (39,24), (37,26), (35,28), (33,30), (31,32), (29,34), (27,36), (25,38), (23,40), (21,42), (19,44), (17,46)
That's 17 more steps to reach the end of the gray dot path. But the blue creature is at (11-19, 44-52), so the last gray dot (17,46) is near the blue creature.
Let me batch all these clicks!

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking on a gray dot along the path moves the purple square to that position. The old position becomes a gray dot. The purple square moved from (53,10) to (51,12). The bottom bar decreases by 2 each action (now 58).
Goal model: Move the purple square along the diagonal path all the way to the blue creature at the top-right.
Action model: Click on successive gray dots along the path to advance the purple square.
Plan: Batch-click all remaining gray dots along the path toward the blue creature: (49,14), (47,16), ..., (17,46).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The path positions (sorted from bottom-left to top-right):
# (57,6), (55,8), (53,10), (51,12), (49,14), (47,16), (45,18), (43,20), (41,22), (39,24), (37,26), (35,28), (33,30), (31,32), (29,34), (27,36), (25,38), (23,40), (21,42), (19,44), (17,46)
# Purple square is at (51,12). Next targets toward blue creature:
path_targets = [
    (49, 14), (47, 16), (45, 18), (43, 20), (41, 22), (39, 24), (37, 26),
    (35, 28), (33, 30), (31, 32), (29, 34), (27, 36), (25, 38), (23, 40),
    (21, 42), (19, 44), (17, 46)
]

# Build action list
actions = [{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c} for r, c in path_targets]

# Execute all at once
action(actions)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

    print(f"  id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Bottom bar
bar = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 50]
print(f"\nBottom bar: {bar[0]['pixels'] if bar else 'N/A'} pixels")

# Gray dots
gray = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'G' and n['pixels'] == 1]
positions = sorted([(n['boundary'][0][0], n['boundary'][0][1]) for n in gray])
print(f"\nGray dots: {len(gray)}")
print(f"Positions: {positions}")

# Check for white cross
white_small = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] < 10]
print(f"\nSmall white objects: {len(white_small)}")
for n in white_small:
    print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The purple square moved from (52,9)-(54,11) to (50,11)-(52,13). That's a shift of (-2, +2) - moving up and right along the diagonal path toward the blue creature!
Also note that the gray dot that was at (51,12) is now gone, and a new gray dot appeared at (53,10) - the position where the purple square was before. So the purple square replaced the gray dot at (51,12), and the old position became a gray dot.
So the mechanic is:
1. Click on a gray dot along the path to move the purple square to that position
2. The purple square swaps with the gray dot (old position becomes a gray dot)
3. The bottom bar decreases by 2 each action
The path goes from bottom-left to top-right. The purple square is now at (51,12) approximately. The next gray dot toward the blue creature would be (49,14).
Let me continue clicking on each successive gray dot to move the purple square all the way to the blue creature. The path positions are:
(57,6), (55,8), (53,10), (49,14), (47,16), (45,18), (43,20), (41,22), (39,24), (37,26), (35,28), (33,30), (31,32), (29,34), (27,36), (25,38), (23,40), (21,42), (19,44), (17,46)
The purple square is currently at (51,12) which corresponds to the position between (53,10) and (49,14). The next target is (49,14).
Wait, let me reconsider. The purple square boundary is [[50, 11], [50, 13], [52, 13], [52, 11]], so it's a 3x3 square centered at (51, 12). The gray dot positions now include (53,10) which was the previous position of the purple square.
So the pattern is:
- Purple square was at (53,10) center
- I clicked on (51,12) which was a gray dot
- Purple square moved to (51,12) center
- The old position (53,10) became a gray dot
Now the purple square is at (51,12). The next gray dot toward the blue creature is (49,14). Let me click on that.
Actually, I should batch multiple clicks to be efficient. The path from current position to the blue creature goes through:
(49,14), (47,16), (45,18), (43,20), (41,22), (39,24), (37,26), (35,28), (33,30), (31,32), (29,34), (27,36), (25,38), (23,40), (21,42), (19,44), (17,46)
That's 17 more steps to reach the end of the gray dot path. But the blue creature is at (11-19, 44-52), so the last gray dot (17,46) is near the blue creature.
Let me batch all these clicks!

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking on a gray dot along the path moves the purple square to that position. The old position becomes a gray dot. The purple square moved from (53,10) to (51,12). The bottom bar decreases by 2 each action (now 58).
Goal model: Move the purple square along the diagonal path all the way to the blue creature at the top-right.
Action model: Click on successive gray dots along the path to advance the purple square.
Plan: Batch-click all remaining gray dots along the path toward the blue creature: (49,14), (47,16), ..., (17,46).

# Purple square is at (51,12). Next targets toward blue creature:

    (49, 14), (47, 16), (45, 18), (43, 20), (41, 22), (39, 24), (37, 26),

]

# Execute all at once
action(actions)
